raw_abstract: ABSTRACT WeChat exemplifies a unique entanglement of private and public data infrastructures. As China's “super app,” WeChat (微信) integrates seamlessly into daily life, enabling communication, shopping, utility payments, and access to government services. Prior research has thoroughly explored how WeChat has supported the information censorship regime in China. However, WeChat's impact on public security capabilities and services has not been previously examined. To address this gap, this article traces how Tencent (腾讯)—the company behind WeChat and one of the biggest communications companies in China—began working with public security agencies in China to develop public security WeChat and even supply WeChat‐supported policing services. This article draws on a multi‐modal data set of government procurement records ( n = 53), supplemented with digitally archived news articles. Integrated into profitable surveillance assemblages, public security WeChat and WeChat policing have evolved into a plug‐and‐play components of digital public‐private infrastructures. The analysis shows how WeChat went from supporting the Party‐state's information control efforts to becoming an integrated module of China's public security technologies.
abstract_en: ABSTRACT WeChat exemplifies a unique entanglement of private and public data infrastructures. As China's “super app,” WeChat (微信) integrates seamlessly into daily life, enabling communication, shopping, utility payments, and access to government services. Prior research has thoroughly explored how WeChat has supported the information censorship regime in China. However, WeChat's impact on public security capabilities and services has not been previously examined. To address this gap, this article traces how Tencent (腾讯)—the company behind WeChat and one of the biggest communications companies in China—began working with public security agencies in China to develop public security WeChat and even supply WeChat‐supported policing services. This article draws on a multi‐modal data set of government procurement records ( n = 53), supplemented with digitally archived news articles. Integrated into profitable surveillance assemblages, public security WeChat and WeChat policing have evolved into a plug‐and‐play components of digital public‐private infrastructures. The analysis shows how WeChat went from supporting the Party‐state's information control efforts to becoming an integrated module of China's public security technologies.
abstract_zh: 摘要 微信体现了私人和公共数据基础设施的独特交织。作为中国的“超级应用”，微信无缝融入日常生活，支持通信、购物、公用事业支付和政府服务访问。先前的研究已深入探讨了微信如何支持中国的信息审查制度。然而，微信对公共安全能力和服务的影响尚未被研究。为填补这一空白，本文追溯了腾讯（微信背后的公司，中国最大的通信公司之一）如何开始与中国公共安全机构合作，开发公共安全微信，甚至提供微信支持的警务服务。本文利用政府采购记录（n = 53）的多模态数据集，辅以数字存档的新闻文章。公共安全微信和微信警务已融入盈利的监控组合，演变为数字公私基础设施的即插即用组件。分析显示，微信如何从支持党国的信息控制努力，转变为中国公共安全技术的集成模块。
plain_summary_zh: 这篇文章研究微信如何从日常聊天应用变成警务工具。它发现腾讯公司与中国警方合作，开发了专门用于公共安全的微信功能，比如监控和执法服务。通过分析政府购买记录和新闻，文章显示微信已成为公私合作的一部分，帮助加强社会监控。简单说，微信不只是用来发消息，还在帮助警察工作。
plain_summary_en: This article examines how WeChat has evolved from a daily messaging app into a tool for policing. It finds that Tencent, the company behind WeChat, has collaborated with Chinese police to develop features for public security, such as surveillance and law enforcement services. By analyzing government purchase records and news articles, the study shows that WeChat has become part of public-private partnerships, aiding in social monitoring. In short, WeChat isn't just for chatting; it's also helping police work.
research_direction_zh: 研究数字平台在公共安全领域的角色演变，特别是公私合作如何塑造监控技术和基础设施。
research_direction_en: Investigating the evolving role of digital platforms in public security, focusing on how public-private partnerships shape surveillance technologies and infrastructure.
